title: Why does the Venezuela crisis matter to China? | FT #shorts

transcript.text: So what does China have at stake in the
Venezuela crisis? The first thing is
geopolitical influence. Since 1999 when
Hugo Chavez took power in Venezuela, the
country has been a springboard for China
into Latin America. Today, China's
bilateral trade with Latin America
exceeds 500 billion with China buying
commodities ranging from copper to iron
ore and of course oil. But after it
kidnapped Venezuelan President Nicholas
Maduro, the US has made clear that the
Western Hemisphere is off limits for its
adversaries and that includes China. And
then there's China's aid to Venezuela.
It's unclear if the US will let
Venezuela pay back its loans to China.
China has lent Venezuela about $60
billion under loans for oil deals. It's
estimated that about $10 billion of
these loans are still outstanding.
Donald Trump has indicated that he wants
to control Venezuela's oil flows [music]
indefinitely. That does not bode well
for China's creditors. Finally, Trump's
action in Venezuela also bodess the
question of whether he'll go after
Iran's oil exports, which are also
subject to US sanctions. China is the
world's largest importer of oil. While
Venezuela was a relatively small source
of oil, Iran [music] is much, much
bigger. So any interference in Iran
could cut off China's access to
discounted oil, [music] which could
cause severe disruptions in China's
refining sector. So, while China has
criticized US action in Venezuela as
violating international law, there could
be just one silver lining for Beijing in
all of this. It could [music] make it
easier for Beijing to justify aggression
in its own region, especially in the
South China Sea and around Taiwan.
